content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 .r Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Leon J. Page 
Orange County Counsel 
P.O. Box 1379 
Santa Ana, CA 92702-13 79 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-221 
Dear Mr. Page: 
January 14, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Orange County Board of 
Supervisors regarding Section 1090 and the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including the Public Contract Code or 
common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re 
Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete 
and accurate. 
Please note that after forwarding your request to the Attorney General's Office and the 
Orange County District Attorney's Office, we did not receive a written response from either 
entity. (See Section 1097. l (c)( 4).) Finally, we are required to advise you that the following 
advice is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. 
(See Section 1097.1(c)(5).) 
QUESTIONS 
I. Does Section l 090 prohibit the County of Orange from entering into the Agreement for 
the Provision of Administrative Mental Health Hospital Triage Services (the "Proposed 
Agreement") with the Hospital Association of Southern California (the "Hospital 
Association") where the county will pay for the services with funds received from a state 
commission under a grant and the Hospital Association assisted the county in preparing 
the grant proposal (the "Grant Proposal")? 
2. Is the Hospital Association a "public official" under Section 82048 of the Act for 
purposes of a conflicts analysis under Section 87100? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-15-221 
Page No. 2 
1. No. Section l 090 does not prohibit the county from now entering into the Proposed 
Agreement for mental health services with the Hospital Association, where the grant was 
made by a state agency, the county and the Hospital Association worked together to apply for 
the grant, and in the course of the grant application process it was understood that the Hospital 
Association would perfonn the mental health triage services if the county received the grant 
from the state commission. The Hospital Association's efforts in preparing the Grant Proposal 
and the services it" will provide under the Proposed Agreement are part of one transaction with 
the county that was negotiated by the Hospital Association in its private capacity. 
2. No, the Hospital Association is not a public official under the Act because the tenn 
"consultant'' is defined under Regulation 18700.3 as an individual who makes a governmental 
decision. 
FACTS 
In October 2013 the state Mental Health Services Oversight and Accountability 
Commission (the "State Commission") issued a Request for Application ("RFA") for a grant that 
would fund the hiring of hospital triage personnel for treating people with mental health illnesses 
in need of crisis intervention. Only counties and cities could apply for the grant. The county 
submitted the Grant Proposal in response to the RF A. Because the county docs not own or 
operate any hospitals, if awarded the grant, the county would have to contract with independent 
hospitals or other service providers to implement the tenns of the grant. 
The Hospital Association, a nonprofit trade association that represents thirty hospitals in 
Orange County and has a longstanding relationship with the county in providing health care 
services, worked very closely with county staff to assist in the preparation of the Grant Proposal. 
This work included drafting substantial parts of the Grant Proposal without the county making 
any changes, and reviewing and editing the Grant Proposal to ensure that it met the RF A's 
requirements as well as the Hospital Association's objectives. The county believes that 
collaborating with the Hospital Association in preparing the Grant Proposal was essential to 
preparing a viable proposal. The Hospital Association perfonned the work without receiving any 
compensation. At the very outset of the county's decision to submit a Grant Proposal, the county 
intended to contract with the Hospital Association to perfonn its obligations under the tenns of 
the grant. 
The county was awarded the grant by the State Commission and has entered into an 
agreement with the State Commission (the "Grant Agreement") for a$ 10,250,000 grant over a 
three-year period. The Grant Agreement incorporates the provisions of the RFA and the county's 
Grant Proposal. The Orange County Board of Supervisors has approved the Grant Agreement. 
After being awarded the grant, the county began negotiating with the Hospital 
Association for the "Proposed Agreement" under which the Hospital Association would assume 
the county's obligations under the Grant Agreement. The provisions of the Proposed Agreement 
essentially mirror those of the Grant Agreement that were written in part by the Hospital 


File No. A-15-221 
Page No. 3 
Association. The same personnel of the Hospital Association that assisted in drafting the Grant 
Proposal have been involved in negotiating the Proposed Agreement. The Board of Supervisors 
will soon be considering the Proposed Agreement for approval. 
ANALYSIS 
Sectioll 1090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft ( 1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike 
at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." ( City of Imperial 
Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the 
making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig ( 1996) 48 
Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 
Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." 
Courts have long found that independent contractors that serve in advisory positions that 
have a potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency 
are subject to Section 1090. (See Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 
186 Cal.App.4th 1114, 1124-1125; Schaefer v. Berinstein (1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 278, 291.) 
Section 1090 not only applies to independent contractors and consultants that are individuals but 
also to those that are corporations. (Davis v. Fresno Unified School District (2015) 237 
Cal.App.4th 261.) 
Section 1090 applies to a broad range of actions by a public official other than actually 
approving a contract, including actions involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, 
compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing plans and specifications, and solicitation for bids 
(Millbrae Assn for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222. 237; see 
also Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569), so long as those actions take place in his 
or her official, rather than private capacity. (See, e.g., 80 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 41 (1997); Drexel 
Advice Letter, No. 1-14-075.) While Section 1090 can apply to a person who works for a 
government agency under an independent contract, it only applies when that person participates 
in making an agency contract in his or her official capacity. 
We have previously advised that, based on the above principles, Section 1090 does not 
prohibit a law finn from entering into a contract with a government agency to both advise the 
agency on matters and then subsequently receive additional compensation to litigate the matters so 
long as the firm was acting in its private capacity when negotiating the contract for both services 


File No. A-15-221 
Page No. 4 
with the county. (Pans/..y Advice Letter, No. l-14-096.) In finding that the agreement would not 
violate Section 1090, we said: 
"Section 1090 does not prohibit a private attorney or law firm from making an 
independent contract to perform legal services with a government agency so long as 
the attorney or law firm is acting in its private capacity when negotiating or 
otherwise presenting the contract to the agency for its approval in some form such as 
a bid. However, the question here is whether Section 1090 prohibits the attorney or 
law firm, under the already negotiated contract, from receiving the additional 
compensation to litigate agency cases on which they have provided the agency legal 
advice. The Section 1090 issue arises here presumably because the attorney, after the 
contract is in effect and while acting in his or her official capacity, participates in the 
agency decision that results in the additional compensation pursuant to the contract." 
(Emphasis added.) 
We concluded that there was no Section 1090 violation arising from the payment of the 
additional fees because it did not involve a future separate contract with an outside party in which 
the construction counsel had a financial interest. lnstead the potential for additional compensation 
for litigation services was part of the original contract that the law firm negotiated in its private 
capacity. 
Although the facts are distinguishable, Pans/..y provides guidance in the present case. The 
Hospital Association helped develop the Grant Proposal as part of defining the terms and conditions 
under which it would perform any services in the event that the county was awarded the grant. In 
fact, the RF A required a description oflocal efforts to coordinate and collaborate with hospitals in 
both "developing the grant response and in service activity." At the time the parties collaborated on 
the Grant Proposal, both parties intended and contemplated that the county would enter into the 
Proposed Agreement with the Hospital Association. We believe the situation to be similar to Pans/..y 
in that the Proposed Agreement was the very subject of what was essentially negotiated by the 
parties in developing the Grant Proposal. As in Pans/..y, the compensation the Hospital Association 
will receive under the Proposed Agreement is not received under a "future separate contract with an 
outside party," but is received in accordance with the terms of the Grant Proposal in which the 
Hospital Association collaborated on in its private capacity. The fact that the Hospital Association 
was not paid for its efforts on the Grant Proposal supports the conclusion that the Proposed 
Agreement and the earlier work are part of a single transaction. 
Here the Hospital Association worked together with the county to present a proposal and 
attempt to receive a grant from the state for mental health services. The county was working with 
the Hospital Association as the only viable candidate to fulfill the proposal. And the decision as to 
whether the county received the grant was made by a separate entity, the State Commission. 
We find that, under the unique facts you have provided, the county and the Hospital 
Association may enter into the Proposed Agreement without violating Section 1090. 


Political Reform A ct 
File No. A-15-221 
Page No. 5 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. (Section 87103.) "Public official" is defined in Section 82048 as "every 
member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency." The only 
category that may apply to the Hospital Association is "consultant." Under Regulation 18700.3, 
however, "consultant" is defined, in relevant part, as an individual who makes a governmental 
decision. Because the Hospital Association is a nonprofit entity and not an individual, it is not a 
"public official" under the Act. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
eral Counsel 
na Joyce 
I, Legal Division 
VJ:jgl
